sustainability_reasoning: Modern harbor infrastructure addresses a structural constraint — post-catch losses, high transaction costs, and lack of value-addition facilities that suppress fisher incomes. Risk: underutilization if complementary services (ice supply, skilled labor, credit) are not coordinated.

promise_text_ta: khj« khåa éiyæš tH§f¥gL« Orš 500 è£luhf ca®¤Â tH§f¥gL«. é‰gid tç u¤J brŒa¥g£l vçbghUŸ khj« 4000 è£l®
impact_mechanism: Reduces input costs for fishing by subsidizing nets (new and replacement) and provides direct income support for net-based fishing activities.

promise_components.component: Monthly subsidy of ₹500 for fishing nets
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: subsidy amount (₹500/month). Missing: (a) scope — is this a per-fisher or per-net subsidy? (b) number of beneficiary fishers or nets; (c) basis for ₹500 (is it 50% of net cost, or full cost of maintenance?). Assume it applies to all fishing households (~1–1.5 lakh nets in active use across TN; data unavailable). Annual cost at 1.2 lakh nets: ₹500 × 12 × 1.2 lakh = ₹720 cr/year.

promise_components.component: ₹4,000 monthly allowance for nets distributed for fishing activities
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: monthly allowance (₹4,000). Missing: (a) is this in addition to or instead of the ₹500 subsidy? (b) what is the trigger — receipt of a newly distributed net? (c) how many nets are 'distributed' annually? Interpretation conflict: if both apply to same fisher, annual benefit = (₹500 + ₹4,000) × 12 = ₹54,000/fisher. If ₹4,000 is a one-time payment per distributed net, cost depends on distribution rate. Assume ₹4,000 is a six-month allowance per new/replacement net distributed; assume 30,000 nets distributed annually (data unavailable): ₹4,000 × 30,000 × 2 (biannual) = ₹240 cr/year.

fiscal_cost_note: Total estimated cost: ₹720 cr (₹500 monthly subsidy) + ₹240 cr (₹4,000 allowance) = ₹960 crore/year. This is 0.52% of discretionary budget — affordable. However, promise contains ambiguity: whether both subsidies apply to same beneficiary is unclear. If they do, cost could reach ₹1,200+ cr/year. Implementation risk: high — requires robust fisher registry, targeting mechanism, and monthly cash transfer system to prevent fraud.
sustainability_reasoning: Net subsidies reduce immediate input costs, providing cash relief. However, they do not address root causes: low catch volumes (due to overfishing/climate), lack of alternative income sources, or market access constraints. Subsidy approach is recurring-cost intensive and does not build fisher resilience. Structural alternative: investment in sustainable fishing methods, aquaculture diversification, or skill training for non-fishing occupations.

fiscal_cost_note: Annual cost: data unavailable — no mechanism or unit cost specified. If interpreted as ₹500/month employment allowance to 50,000 family members = ₹30 cr/year (0.016% of discretionary budget). If interpreted as job placement/training support (~₹50,000 per placement) to 10,000 beneficiaries = ₹50 cr one-time + ₹5 cr/year admin. Without clarity on design, fiscal assessment impossible.
sustainability_reasoning: Employment assistance is a real income support for a vulnerable cohort but is transactional (one-time or time-limited transfer) rather than structural. Does not build skills, address skill-job mismatch, or improve employability in sustainable way. Depends on identifying eligible households (verification cost high), designing delivery mechanism, and annual budget availability. Root cause — lack of skills or labour-market access — is not addressed. High implementation risk because no delivery mechanism, eligibility criteria, or funding source is specified; promise is aspirational without operational detail.
